nativeRange.habitat: Coral reefs, rubble substrates, sandy bottoms, seagrass beds; typically found at depths of 3–70 m in clear tropical waters

diet.notes: Generally requires live foods initially — small crustaceans such as black worms, brine shrimp, mysis shrimp, and amphipods are typically accepted first. May eventually adapt to frozen meaty foods; patience and target feeding are often advised. Has been known to forage sand for worms, small invertebrates, and occasionally very small fish when food is scarce.

avoidWith: fast-eating species that outcompete at feeding time

summary: A striking black-and-white striped drum with a tall elongated dorsal fin, generally considered a docile, shy species best kept in a well-established reef tank with ample hiding places and live or frozen meaty foods.
careNotes:
The High-Hat (Pareques acuminatus) is a nocturnal species that tends to hide extensively during the day, becoming more active when tank lighting dims. It is generally advised to provide numerous caves and crevices among live rock to help it feel secure. Feeding can be challenging — this species has been reported to initially demand live foods such as black worms, brine shrimp, mysis, krill, and copepods, and may take considerable time to accept frozen or prepared alternatives; target feeding with a pipette or turkey baster is often recommended. A well-established tank with a healthy population of microfauna (copepods, amphipods) is considered beneficial for long-term success.

Can be kept singly or as a pair; pairs introduced simultaneously have been known to coexist well. Generally peaceful but may consume very small ornamental shrimp or tiny fish if given the opportunity. Its slow, deliberate feeding style means it tends to lose out to faster-eating tankmates — special feeding attention is advisable in mixed communities.

Minimum tank size is disputed across sources: LiveAquaria recommends 70 gallons (approximately 265 L), while specialist reef sources (reefapp.net, fishy.wiki) recommend 700 L (approximately 185 gallons). This is a greater than 2× spread; the value used here (500 L) is a cautious average biased toward the larger recommendation, rounded to a common aquarium size. Reviewer should confirm an appropriate minimum based on the intended setup.

Some older sources list this species under the synonym Equetus acuminatus; Pareques acuminatus is the currently accepted name. Order placement also varies by authority: Acanthuriformes (per Fishes of the World, 5th ed.) and Perciformes (per FishBase/GBIF) are both cited; Acanthuriformes is used here per the most recent systematic treatment.

RESOLVED 2026-06-03: The tank minimum size dispute (LiveAquaria 70 gal/265 L vs. specialist sources 700 L) was reviewed against FishBase and Wikipedia. FishBase does not provide captive tank size guidance. Wikipedia similarly contains no aquarium care detail. The source split remains 2 vs 2 (LiveAquaria + Saltwater Aqua-Fish at 265 L; ReefApp + Fishy.wiki at 700 L), but the two specialist reef-hobby sources (ReefApp, Fishy.wiki) independently and consistently cite 700 L — a figure likely grounded in the fish's adult size (23 cm TL), active bottom-foraging behavior, and need for territorial separation. The general retail sources at 265 L are considered understated for long-term husbandry. Minimum tank volume updated from 500 L to 700 L, matching the specialist consensus. Minimum tank length updated from 120 cm to 150 cm to match a typical 700 L footprint.

additional.name: ReefApp Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size, temperature, salinity, tank volume, native range, and diet. Tank volume recommendation of 700 L differs significantly from LiveAquaria's 70 gal / 265 L; average biased toward larger value used (500 L). Temperature max of 26°C consistent with LiveAquaria upper range.

additional.name: Fishy.Wiki
additional.notes: Confirmed adult size 23 cm, temperature 22.2–25.6°C, pH 8.1–8.4, salinity 1.020–1.025, kH 8–12, tank minimum 700 L. Consistent with reefapp.net on specialist parameters.

additional.name: Saltwater Aqua-Fish
additional.notes: Confirmed 23 cm adult size; temperature 23–26°C; pH 8.0–8.3; SG 1.020–1.025. Tank size 265 L (70 gal) stated, consistent with LiveAquaria. Temperament listed as semi-aggressive/territorial — diverges from peaceful consensus of other three sources; majority (peaceful) used.

additional.name: Wikipedia
additional.notes: Taxonomy cross-check: order Acanthuriformes (Fishes of the World, 5th ed.) used; distribution confirmed Western Atlantic from North Carolina to Venezuela. Synonym Equetus acuminatus noted.
